In 2004, It was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Yoshino Mikumari-jinja
吉野水分神社 | image = Yoshino-Mikumari Shrine01.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall is an Important Cultural Asset | width = | type = | dedication = [[Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami]] | founded = Unknown, possibly 806 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 1612 [[Mount Yoshino|Yoshino-yama]], [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino-chō]] [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Yoshino Mikumari Shrine'''|吉野水分神社|Yoshino Mikumari-jinja|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on [[Mount Yoshino]] in [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino district]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. The Shrine is dedicated to ''[[mikumari]]'', a female [[Shinto]] [[kami]] associated with water. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in Japan. The [[honden]], an [[Important Cultural Property]]，is 9 ''[[ken]]'' long and 2 ken wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style with bark shingles, it has a 1x1 ken unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. On each side of this central unit there is a [[dormer]] [[gable]].{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=1 January 2010}} In 2004, It was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. The ''[[honden]]'', an [[Important Cultural Property]]，is 9 ''[[ken]]'' long and 2 ken wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style with bark shingles, it has a 1x1 ken unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. On each side of this central unit there is a [[dormer]] [[gable]].{{cite web|ttp://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=1 January 2010}} In 2004, It was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. 吉野水分神社 | image = Yoshino Mikumari Shrine Main Hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is an Important Cultural Property | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|14|N|135|52|23|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami|sv|Mikumari-no-Kami}} (main deity) | founded_by = | established = Unknown, possibly 806 | date_destroyed = | location = 1612 [[Mount Yoshino|Yoshino-yama]], [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino-chō]] [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Tamayori hime statue detail.JPG|thumb|272px|right|[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] (princess Tamayori)]] [[File:votive-offerings-mikumarijinja-yoshino.jpg|thumb|272px|right|Votive offerings]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshino Mikumari Shrine'''|吉野水分神社|Yoshino Mikumari-jinja|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on [[Mount Yoshino]] in [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino district]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|WD=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). A wooden statue of the deity Tamayori hime is registered as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. == Shrine buildings == The present-day buildings go back to 1605, when [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] rebuilt the shrine, as his father [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] once had prayed here for a son and successor. The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. The three resulting edifices all lie under the same bark roof, which has three [[dormer]] [[gable]]s.{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=1 January 2010}} In 2004, the shrine was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] {{Commons category|Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. 吉野水分神社 | image = Yoshino Mikumari Shrine Main Hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is an Important Cultural Property | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|14|N|135|52|23|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami|sv|Mikumari-no-Kami}} (main deity) | founded_by = | established = Unknown, possibly 806 | date_destroyed = | location = 1612 [[Mount Yoshino|Yoshino-yama]], [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino-chō]] [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Tamayori hime statue detail.JPG|thumb|272px|right|[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] (princess Tamayori)]] [[File:votive-offerings-mikumarijinja-yoshino.jpg|thumb|272px|right|Votive offerings]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshino Mikumari Shrine'''|吉野水分神社|Yoshino Mikumari-jinja|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on [[Mount Yoshino]] in [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino district]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|WD=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). A wooden statue of the deity Tamayori hime is registered as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. == Shrine buildings == The present-day buildings go back to 1605, when [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] rebuilt the shrine, as his father [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] once had prayed here for a son and successor. The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. The three resulting edifices all lie under the same bark roof, which has three [[dormer]] [[gable]]s.{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=1 January 2010}} In 2004, the shrine was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] {{Commons category|Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{Refend}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{Refend}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia25]] [[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia25]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

(1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|WD=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). A wooden statue of the deity Tamayori hime is registered as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. == Shrine buildings == The present-day buildings go back to 1605, when [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] rebuilt the shrine, as his father [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] once had prayed here for a son and successor. The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. The three resulting edifices all lie under the same bark roof, which has three [[dormer]] [[gable]]s.{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=1 January 2010}} In 2004, the shrine was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] {{Commons category|Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|WD=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|WD=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). A wooden statue of the deity Tamayori hime is registered as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. == Shrine buildings == The present-day buildings go back to 1605, when [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] rebuilt the shrine, as his father [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] once had prayed here for a son and successor. The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. The three resulting edifices all lie under the same bark roof, which has three [[dormer]] [[gable]]s.{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=1 January 2010}} In 2004, the shrine was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] {{Commons Category|Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. 吉野水分神社 | image = Yoshino Mikumari Shrine Main Hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is an Important Cultural Property | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|14|N|135|52|23|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami|sv|Mikumari-no-Kami}} (main deity) | founded_by = | established = Unknown, possibly 806 | date_destroyed = | location = 1612 [[Mount Yoshino|Yoshino-yama]], [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino-chō]] [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Tamayori hime statue detail.JPG|thumb|272px|right|[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] (princess Tamayori)]] [[File:votive-offerings-mikumarijinja-yoshino.jpg|thumb|272px|right|Votive offerings]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshino Mikumari Shrine'''|吉野水分神社|Yoshino Mikumari-jinja|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on [[Mount Yoshino]] in [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino district]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|WD=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). A wooden statue of the deity Tamayori hime is registered as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. == Shrine buildings == The present-day buildings go back to 1605, when [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] rebuilt the shrine, as his father [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] once had prayed here for a son and successor. The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. The three resulting edifices all lie under the same bark roof, which has three [[dormer]] [[gable]]s.{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=1 January 2010}} In 2004, the shrine was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] {{Commons category|Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). revision.text:
{{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yoshino Mikumari-jinja
吉野水分神社 | image = Yoshino Mikumari Shrine Main Hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is an Important Cultural Property | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|14|N|135|52|23|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami|sv|Mikumari-no-Kami}} (main deity) | founded_by = | established = Unknown, possibly 806 | date_destroyed = | location = 1612 [[Mount Yoshino|Yoshino-yama]], [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino-chō]] [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Tamayori hime statue detail.JPG|thumb|272px|right|[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] (princess Tamayori)]] [[File:votive-offerings-mikumarijinja-yoshino.jpg|thumb|272px|right|Votive offerings]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshino Mikumari Shrine'''|吉野水分神社|Yoshino Mikumari-jinja|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on [[Mount Yoshino]] in [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino district]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|qid=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). A wooden statue of the deity Tamayori hime is registered as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. == Shrine buildings == The present-day buildings go back to 1605, when [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] rebuilt the shrine, as his father [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] once had prayed here for a son and successor. The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. The three resulting edifices all lie under the same bark roof, which has three [[dormer]] [[gable]]s.{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=1 January 2010|archive-date=28 December 2005|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|url-status=dead}} In 2004, the shrine was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] {{Commons category|Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{Refend}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]]

(1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|WD=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). A wooden statue of the deity Tamayori hime is registered as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. == Shrine buildings == The present-day buildings go back to 1605, when [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] rebuilt the shrine, as his father [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] once had prayed here for a son and successor. The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. The three resulting edifices all lie under the same bark roof, which has three [[dormer]] [[gable]]s.{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=1 January 2010}} In 2004, the shrine was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] {{Commons category|Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{Refend}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]]]] [[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]]]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]]]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

{{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yoshino Mikumari-jinja
吉野水分神社 | image = Yoshino Mikumari Shrine Main Hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is an Important Cultural Property | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|14|N|135|52|23|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami|sv|Mikumari-no-Kami}} (main deity) | founded_by = | established = Unknown, possibly 806 | date_destroyed = | location = 1612 [[Mount Yoshino|Yoshino-yama]], [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino-chō]] [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Tamayori hime statue detail.JPG|thumb|272px|right|[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] (princess Tamayori)]] [[File:votive-offerings-mikumarijinja-yoshino.jpg|thumb|272px|right|Votive offerings]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshino Mikumari Shrine'''|吉野水分神社|Yoshino Mikumari-jinja|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on [[Mount Yoshino]] in [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino district]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|qid=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). A wooden statue of the deity Tamayori hime is registered as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. == Shrine buildings == The present-day buildings go back to 1605, when [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] rebuilt the shrine, as his father [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] once had prayed here for a son and successor. The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. The three resulting edifices all lie under the same bark roof, which has three [[dormer]] [[gable]]s.{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=1 January 2010|archive-date=28 December 2005|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|url-status=dead}} In 2004, the shrine was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] {{Commons category|Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{Refend}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] {{wikidata link|Q3529714}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[ca:Santuari Yoshino Mikumari]][[commons:Category:Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja]][[cs:Svatyně Jošino Mikumari]][[de:Mikumari-Schrein (Yoshino)]][[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yoshino Mikumari]][[eu:Yoshino Mikumari santutegia]][[fa:معبد یاشینو میکوماری]][[fr:Yoshino Mikumari-jinja]][[it:Santuario di Yoshino]][[ja:吉野水分神社]][[lt:Mikumari maldykla]][[tr:Yoshino Mikumari Tapınağı]][[zh:吉野水分神社]] {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yoshino Mikumari-jinja
吉野水分神社 | image = Yoshino Mikumari Shrine Main Hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is an Important Cultural Property | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|14|N|135|52|23|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami|sv|Mikumari-no-Kami}} (main deity) | founded_by = | established = Unknown, possibly 806 | date_destroyed = | location = 1612 [[Mount Yoshino|Yoshino-yama]], [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino-chō]] [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Tamayori hime statue detail.JPG|thumb|272px|right|[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] (princess Tamayori)]] [[File:votive-offerings-mikumarijinja-yoshino.jpg|thumb|272px|right|Votive offerings]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshino Mikumari Shrine'''|吉野水分神社|Yoshino Mikumari-jinja|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on [[Mount Yoshino]] in [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino district]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|qid=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). A wooden statue of the deity Tamayori hime is registered as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. == Shrine buildings == The present-day buildings go back to 1605, when [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] rebuilt the shrine, as his father [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] once had prayed here for a son and successor. The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. The three resulting edifices all lie under the same bark roof, which has three [[dormer]] [[gable]]s.{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=1 January 2010|archive-date=28 December 2005|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|url-status=dead}} In 2004, the shrine was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] {{Commons category|Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. [[ca:Santuari Yoshino Mikumari]][[commons:Category:Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja]][[cs:Svatyně Jošino Mikumari]][[de:Mikumari-Schrein (Yoshino)]][[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yoshino Mikumari]][[eu:Yoshino Mikumari santutegia]][[fa:معبد یاشینو میکوماری]][[fr:Yoshino Mikumari-jinja]][[it:Santuario di Yoshino]][[ja:吉野水分神社]][[lt:Mikumari maldykla]][[tr:Yoshino Mikumari Tapınağı]][[zh:吉野水分神社]] [[ca:Santuari Yoshino Mikumari]][[commons:Category:Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja]][[cs:Svatyně Jošino Mikumari]][[de:Mikumari-Schrein (Yoshino)]][[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yoshino Mikumari]][[eu:Yoshino Mikumari santutegia]][[fa:معبد یاشینو میکوماری]][[fr:Yoshino Mikumari-jinja]][[it:Santuario di Yoshino]][[ja:吉野水分神社]][[lt:Mikumari maldykla]][[tr:Yoshino Mikumari Tapınağı]][[zh:吉野水分神社]] {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yoshino Mikumari-jinja
吉野水分神社 | image = Yoshino Mikumari Shrine Main Hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is an Important Cultural Property | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|14|N|135|52|23|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami|sv|Mikumari-no-Kami}} (main deity) | founded_by = | established = Unknown, possibly 806 | date_destroyed = | location = 1612 [[Mount Yoshino|Yoshino-yama]], [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino-chō]] [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Tamayori hime statue detail.JPG|thumb|272px|right|[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] (princess Tamayori)]] [[File:votive-offerings-mikumarijinja-yoshino.jpg|thumb|272px|right|Votive offerings]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshino Mikumari Shrine'''|吉野水分神社|Yoshino Mikumari-jinja|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on [[Mount Yoshino]] in [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino district]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|qid=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). A wooden statue of the deity Tamayori hime is registered as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. == Shrine buildings == The present-day buildings go back to 1605, when [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] rebuilt the shrine, as his father [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] once had prayed here for a son and successor. The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. The three resulting edifices all lie under the same bark roof, which has three [[dormer]] [[gable]]s.{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=1 January 2010|archive-date=28 December 2005|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|url-status=dead}} In 2004, the shrine was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] {{Commons category|Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. revision.text:
[[ca:Santuari Yoshino Mikumari]][[co:commons:Category:Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja]][[cs:Svatyně Jošino Mikumari]][[de:Mikumari-Schrein (Yoshino)]][[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yoshino Mikumari]][[eu:Yoshino Mikumari santutegia]][[fa:معبد یاشینو میکوماری]][[fr:Yoshino Mikumari-jinja]][[it:Santuario di Yoshino]][[ja:吉野水分神社]][[lt:Mikumari maldykla]][[tr:Yoshino Mikumari Tapınağı]][[zh:吉野水分神社]] [[ca:Santuari Yoshino Mikumari]][[co:commons:Category:Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja]][[cs:Svatyně Jošino Mikumari]][[de:Mikumari-Schrein (Yoshino)]][[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yoshino Mikumari]][[eu:Yoshino Mikumari santutegia]][[fa:معبد یاشینو میکوماری]][[fr:Yoshino Mikumari-jinja]][[it:Santuario di Yoshino]][[ja:吉野水分神社]][[lt:Mikumari maldykla]][[tr:Yoshino Mikumari Tapınağı]][[zh:吉野水分神社]] {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yoshino Mikumari-jinja
吉野水分神社 | image = Yoshino Mikumari Shrine Main Hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is an Important Cultural Property | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|14|N|135|52|23|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami|sv|Mikumari-no-Kami}} (main deity) | founded_by = | established = Unknown, possibly 806 | date_destroyed = | location = 1612 [[Mount Yoshino|Yoshino-yama]], [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino-chō]] [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Tamayori hime statue detail.JPG|thumb|272px|right|[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] (princess Tamayori)]] [[File:votive-offerings-mikumarijinja-yoshino.jpg|thumb|272px|right|Votive offerings]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshino Mikumari Shrine'''|吉野水分神社|Yoshino Mikumari-jinja|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on [[Mount Yoshino]] in [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino district]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|qid=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). A wooden statue of the deity Tamayori hime is registered as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. == Shrine buildings == The present-day buildings go back to 1605, when [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] rebuilt the shrine, as his father [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] once had prayed here for a son and successor. The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. The three resulting edifices all lie under the same bark roof, which has three [[dormer]] [[gable]]s.{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=1 January 2010|archive-date=28 December 2005|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|url-status=dead}} In 2004, the shrine was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] {{Commons category|Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{Refend}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{wikidata link|Q3529714}} [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Pages likely having an accidental overwite in their history that shrank them]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:World Heritage Sites in Japan]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

[[ca:Santuari Yoshino Mikumari]][[co:commons:Category:Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja]][[cs:Svatyně Jošino Mikumari]][[de:Mikumari-Schrein (Yoshino)]][[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yoshino Mikumari]][[eu:Yoshino Mikumari santutegia]][[fa:معبد یاشینو میکوماری]][[fr:Yoshino Mikumari-jinja]][[it:Santuario di Yoshino]][[ja:吉野水分神社]][[lt:Mikumari maldykla]][[tr:Yoshino Mikumari Tapınağı]][[zh:吉野水分神社]] [[ca:Santuari Yoshino Mikumari]][[co:commons:Category:Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja]][[cs:Svatyně Jošino Mikumari]][[de:Mikumari-Schrein (Yoshino)]][[en:Yoshino Mikumari Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yoshino Mikumari]][[eu:Yoshino Mikumari santutegia]][[fa:معبد یاشینو میکوماری]][[fr:Yoshino Mikumari-jinja]][[it:Santuario di Yoshino]][[ja:吉野水分神社]][[lt:Mikumari maldykla]][[tr:Yoshino Mikumari Tapınağı]][[zh:吉野水分神社]] {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yoshino Mikumari-jinja
吉野水分神社 | image = Yoshino Mikumari Shrine Main Hall 001.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main hall, is an Important Cultural Property | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|21|14|N|135|52|23|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-mikumari-no-kami|sv|Mikumari-no-Kami}} (main deity) | founded_by = | established = Unknown, possibly 806 | date_destroyed = | location = 1612 [[Mount Yoshino|Yoshino-yama]], [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino-chō]] [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara-ken]] | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Tamayori hime statue detail.JPG|thumb|272px|right|[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-hime]] (princess Tamayori)]] [[File:votive-offerings-mikumarijinja-yoshino.jpg|thumb|272px|right|Votive offerings]] {{nihongo|'''Yoshino Mikumari Shrine'''|吉野水分神社|Yoshino Mikumari-jinja|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located on [[Mount Yoshino]] in [[Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino district]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].Richard, Ponsonby-Fane. (1964) '' Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 300-307. It is closely associated with [[Emperor Go-Daigo]]. == The Shrine’s deity == The Shrine is dedicated to ''{{ill|mikumari|qid=Q11129547}}'', a female [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' associated with water, fertility and safe birth. Yoshino Mikumari Shrine is one of four important ''mikumari'' shrines in the former province Yamato. The shrine also houses six ''kami'' that are more or less related to mikumari (Takami-musubi-no-kami, Sukuna-hiko-no-kami, Mikogami, Ama-tsu-hiko-hi-no-ninigi-no-mikoto, Tamayori-hime-no-mikoto, and Yorozu-hata-toyo-akitsushi-hime-no-mikoto). A wooden statue of the deity Tamayori hime is registered as a [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure of Japan]]. == Shrine buildings == The present-day buildings go back to 1605, when [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] rebuilt the shrine, as his father [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] once had prayed here for a son and successor. The main hall (''[[honden]]''), an [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]], is an unusual structure 9 ''[[ken (architecture)|ken]]'' long and 2 ''ken'' wide. Built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style, it has however an independent 1x1 ''ken'' unit in the ''[[kasuga-zukuri]]'' style at the center. The three resulting edifices all lie under the same bark roof, which has three [[dormer]] [[gable]]s.{{cite web|url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|title=Yoshino Mikumari Jinja|work=Cultural Properties Database|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=ja|access-date=1 January 2010|archive-date=28 December 2005|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20051228033527/http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index.asp|url-status=dead}} In 2004, the shrine was designated as part of a [[UNESCO]] [[World Heritage Site]] under the name ''[[Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range]]''. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] {{Commons category|Yoshino-Mikumari-jinja}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959).
